summary: Stancil argues that the January 6th attack and the GameStop saga reveal the same structural danger: social media enables fringe groups to reach critical mass quickly, turning online narratives into real-world destabilization. Because scale and speed are the product, even well-intentioned movements can spiral, leaving society on a runaway train with only two options—submit to the ride or dismantle the track.
money_quotes: All it takes is social media, at a global scale, operating as intended.
It turns out, to make the world wobble, a lot fewer people have to jump than we thought.
Off we’ll go, careening down an uncertain track on a runaway train captained by a rotating cast of hijackers—some who mean well, some who don’t, and most of whom just think it’s funny.
At this point, we have two choices: Give in to the ride, or dismantle the entire track.
Perhaps, just because we can build it, doesn’t mean we should.
key_insight: The core risk isn’t misinformation or bad actors alone, but the scale of social media itself, which reliably converts fringe energy into real-world disruption.
